messages.content:
Both the pre-verbal and the post-verbal adverb in Wu dialect can be present in the embedded clauses, for instance, in a complement clause of a verb (see 4a) and in a sentential subject (see 4b). For ease of exposition, brackets are used to represent the three situations: pre-verbal, post-verbal and double-adverb.

The same situation is observed with the adverb ɕi 11 ‘first’ in Oujiang Wu, another sub-variety of Wu dialects, as shown in (5). The adverb ɕi 11 can appear in a post-verbal position as in (5a) or a pre-verbal position as in (5b). Similarly, the adverb can co-occur in both pre-verbal and post-verbal positions, as shown in (5c), which might convey a more urgent mood of the speaker. The semantic interpretation of these three sentences is the same, just like the case of Hangzhou Wu in (1). Given these subtle differences in pragmatics, we take the cases like (1) or (5) as pseudo-optionality.

In other words, the presence of both pre-verbal and post-verbal adverbs in Wu dialect can occur in various grammatical contexts, such as complement clauses and sentential subjects. This phenomenon is also observed in the adverb "ɕi 11 'first" in Oujiang Wu, suggesting that it is not limited to a specific adverb but is a more general feature of Wu dialects. The co-occurrence of pre-verbal and post-verbal positions may convey a sense of urgency or emphasis, but the overall semantic interpretation remains the same. These cases of variability in adverb placement are considered pseudo-optionality due to their subtle differences in pragmatics.

Both the pre-verbal and post-verbal adverbs in Wu dialect can be present in embedded clauses, for instance, in a complement clause of a verb (see 4a) and in a sentential subject (see 4b). For ease of exposition, brackets are used to represent the three situations: pre-verbal, post-verbal, and double adverb.

The same situation is observed with the adverb "ɕi 11 'first" in Oujiang Wu, another sub-variety of Wu dialects, as shown in (5). The adverb "ɕi 11" can appear in a post-verbal position as in (5a) or a pre-verbal position as in (5b). Similarly, the adverb can co-occur in both pre-verbal and post-verbal positions, as shown in (5c), which might convey a more urgent mood of the speaker. The semantic interpretation of these three sentences is the same, just like the case of Hangzhou Wu in (1). Given these subtle differences in pragmatics, we consider cases like (1) or (5) as pseudo-optionality.

Both the pre-verbal and the post-verbal adverb in Wu dialect can be present in the embedded clauses, for instance, in a complement clause of a verb (see 4a) and in a sentential subject (see 4b). For ease of exposition, brackets are used to represent the three situations: pre-verbal, post-verbal and double-adverb.

The same situation is observed with the adverb ɕi 11 ‘first’ in Oujiang Wu, another sub-variety of Wu dialects, as shown in (5). The adverb ɕi 11 can appear in a post-verbal position as in (5a) or a pre-verbal position as in (5b). Similarly, the adverb can co-occur in both pre-verbal and post-verbal positions, as shown in (5c), which might convey a more urgent mood of the speaker. The semantic interpretation of these three sentences is the same, just like the case of Hangzhou Wu in (1). Given these subtle differences in pragmatics, we take the cases like (1) or (5) as pseudo-optionality.

Both the pre-verbal and post-verbal adverbs in the Wu dialect can be present in embedded clauses, such as complement clauses of a verb (see 4a) and sentential subjects (see 4b). To simplify the explanation, brackets are used to indicate the three situations: pre-verbal, post-verbal, and double-adverb.

This same phenomenon is observed with the adverb "ɕi 11 'first" in Oujiang Wu, another sub-variety of Wu dialects, as shown in (5). The adverb "ɕi 11" can appear in a post-verbal position, as seen in (5a), or a pre-verbal position, as seen in (5b). Additionally, the adverb can co-occur in both pre-verbal and post-verbal positions, as shown in (5c), which may convey a more urgent mood from the speaker. The semantic interpretation of these three sentences remains the same, similar to the case in Hangzhou Wu (as shown in (1)). Taking into account these subtle differences in pragmatics, we consider cases like (1) or (5) as examples of pseudo-optionality.

Another type of circum-adverbial construction arises when the pre-verbal adverb and the post-verbal adverb have distinct phonetic forms, as shown in (6). In (6a), the adverb gu2 ‘again’ appears in post-verbal position, following the main verb zo43 ‘do’. Interestingly, in (6b), the pre-verbal adverb can be either zɛ 33 ‘again’ or zoŋ21ɕin33 ‘anew’, which has the same semantic meaning as the post-verbal adverb gu2 ‘again’. A subtle difference between zɛ 33and zoŋ21ɕin33 is that zoŋ21ɕin33 is often used in a context in which someone fails in doing something, and that person restarts doing it from the beginning. Importantly, zɛ 33/zoŋ21ɕin33 and gu2 can co-occur, as in (6c). The interpretation of the three sentences is the same, demonstrating an apparent case of pseudo-optionality.

Another type of circum-adverbial construction arises when the pre-verbal adverb and the post-verbal adverb have distinct phonetic forms, as shown in (6). In (6a), the adverb "gu2 'again'" appears in the post-verbal position, following the main verb "zo43 'do'". Interestingly, in (6b), the pre-verbal adverb can be either "zɛ 33 'again'" or "zoŋ21ɕin33 'anew'", both of which have the same semantic meaning as the post-verbal adverb "gu2 'again'". A subtle difference between "zɛ 33" and "zoŋ21ɕin33" is that "zoŋ21ɕin33" is often used in situations where someone fails to do something and then restarts from the beginning. Importantly, "zɛ 33/zoŋ21ɕin33" and "gu2" can co-occur, as seen in (6c). The interpretation of the three sentences remains the same, indicating an apparent case of pseudo-optionality.

Another type of circum-adverbial construction arises when the pre-verbal adverb and the post-verbal adverb have distinct phonetic forms, as shown in (6). In (6a), the adverb "gu2 'again'" appears in the post-verbal position, following the main verb "zo43 'do'". Interestingly, in (6b), the pre-verbal adverb can be either "zɛ 33 'again'" or "zoŋ21ɕin33 'anew'", both of which have the same semantic meaning as the post-verbal adverb "gu2 'again'". A subtle difference between the adverbs "zɛ 33" and "zoŋ21ɕin33" is that "zoŋ21ɕin33" is often used in a context where someone fails to do something and then restarts from the beginning. Importantly, "zɛ 33/zoŋ21ɕin33" and "gu2" can co-occur, as seen in (6c). The interpretation of the three sentences remains the same, demonstrating an apparent case of pseudo-optionality.

The question now pertains to whether any dependency relationship exists between the post-verbal and the pre-verbal adverbs in both cases. In particular, it seems that there might be a derivational relationship between the post-verbal and the pre-verbal adverbs when they are identical in phonetic forms. To investigate this, formal analyses will be conducted in the next section.

The question at hand is whether there is a dependency relationship between the post-verbal and pre-verbal adverbs in both cases. Specifically, it appears that there may be a derivational relationship between the post-verbal and pre-verbal adverbs when they share the same phonetic forms. To explore this further, formal analyses will be conducted in the next section.

First of all, it should be noted that on the surface, circum-adverbial constructions like (5) are similar to another construction, known as circumpositional phrases (see Koopman 2010, Paul 2015), where an element, most likely to be a noun, is surrounded by a preposition and a postposition. In (7), the preposition zài ‘at’ precedes the noun zhuōzi ‘table and shàng ‘on’ follows the noun, (see also Huang, et al., 2009,Paul 2015 for different types of analyses). Nevertheless, both the preposition and the postposition are obligatory in case involving circumpositional phrases, which is crucially different from the current circum-adverbial constructions presented in (1, 2) and (5), in that neither the preverbal nor the postverbal adverb is obligatory in circum-adverbial constructions.

First and foremost, it is important to note that on the surface, circum-adverbial constructions like (5) bear similarities to another construction known as circumpositional phrases (see Koopman 2010, Paul 2015), where an element, typically a noun, is surrounded by a preposition and a postposition. As seen in (7), the preposition "zài 'at'" precedes the noun "zhuōzi 'table'" and the postposition "shàng 'on'" follows the noun (see also Huang et al., 2009, Paul 2015 for different types of analyses). However, what sets circum-adverbial constructions apart from circumpositional phrases is that neither the preverbal nor the postverbal adverb is obligatory, whereas both the preposition and postposition are mandatory in circumpositional phrases. This crucial difference distinguishes circum-adverbial constructions in (1, 2), and (5) from circumpositional phrases.

First and foremost, it is important to note that on the surface, circum-adverbial constructions like (5) bear similarities to another construction known as circumpositional phrases (see Koopman 2010, Paul 2015), where an element, typically a noun, is surrounded by a preposition and a postposition. As seen in (7), the preposition "zài 'at'" precedes the noun "zhuōzi 'table'" and the postposition "shàng 'on'" follows the noun (see also Huang et al., 2009, Paul 2015 for different types of analyses). However, what sets circum-adverbial constructions apart from circumpositional phrases is that neither the preverbal nor the postverbal adverb is obligatory, whereas both the preposition and the postposition are mandatory in circumpositional phrases. This crucial difference distinguishes circum-adverbial constructions in (1, 2), and (5) from circumpositional phrases.

In traditional Chinese linguistics, few analyses of circum-adverbial constructions adopt a syntactic approach. Within the Government and Binding framework, Tang (2006) proposes an analysis for the circum-adverbial constructions in Cantonese. He argues that both pre-verbal and post-verbal adverbs should form a phrase XP, which is projected based the post-verbal adverb. The pre-verbal adverb occupies the specifier or the adjunct position of the (post)adverb phrase. For instance, the Cantonese sentence in (8) exhibits a similar pattern to the circum-adverbial constructions in (5) in Oujiang Wu. Tim1 ‘again’ appears in a post-verbal position while another adverb zoi3 ‘again’ occurs in a pre-verbal position. Both the pre-verbal and the post-verbal adverbs can co-occur, as in (8c). Different from the case in Chinese Wu, the post-verbal adverb tim1 in Cantonese also serves to convey the speaker's mood. As a result, both (8a) and (8c) express an imperative mood of the speaker, while (8b) lacks it due to the absence of tim1 . 

This analysis can successfully capture the fact that in some cases, the post-verbal adverb, as a head, can carry more semantic-pragmatic meaning comparing to the pre-verbal adverb. Different from Tang (2006), we take another approach in this article by examining the derivational relationship between the pre-verbal and post-verbal adverbs, especially for the first type of circum-adverbial construction in which they share the same phonetic form. Within the MP framework (see Chomsky 2000), we will examine the possibility whether the pre-verbal adverb can be derived by Internal Pair-Merge (IPM) of the post-verbal adverb. Richards (2009) argues that Pair-Merge should also be subject to the internal and external dimension just as Set-Merge. In addition, Internal Pair-Merge is agree-less, which can be solely triggered by an optional EPP feature. Following this idea, we can assume that the pre-verbal case (1b) is derived from (1a) through IPM of the adverb Kuɑ 33 ‘fast’. We first show the derivation for the post-verbal case in (1a). Kuɑ 33 is first-merged inside VP. The complex verb tɕʰy 2 -hɒ 33 ‘eat.PERF’ moves from V to v, as shown in (9a). The subject ji13 ‘s/he’ merges at the Spec vP, and the object ja21ve33 ‘dinner’ moves to the outer Spec vP, which is an intermediate landing site for A'-movement, as shown in (9b). Next, the subject ji13 raises to Spec TP. The Sentence-final particle (SFP) de, analyzed as a C head, merges with the TP, projecting a CP, which expresses an affirmative mood of the speaker. The subject ji13 and the object ja21ve33 are then both topicalized, as in (9c). Note that ji13 can also stay in Spec TP without being topicalized, as in (9d).

In traditional Chinese linguistics, there have been few syntactic analyses of circum-adverbial constructions. However, within the Government and Binding framework, Tang (2006) proposes an analysis for circum-adverbial constructions in Cantonese. He argues that both pre-verbal and post-verbal adverbs should form a phrase XP, which is projected based on the post-verbal adverb. The pre-verbal adverb occupies the specifier or adjunct position of the (post)adverb phrase. The Cantonese sentence in (8) exemplifies a similar pattern to the circum-adverbial constructions in Oujiang Wu, as shown in (5). The post-verbal adverb "tim1 'again'" appears while another adverb "zoi3 'again'" occurs in a pre-verbal position. Both the pre-verbal and post-verbal adverbs can co-occur, as seen in (8c). Unlike in Wu dialect, the post-verbal adverb "tim1" in Cantonese also conveys the speaker's mood. As a result, both (8a) and (8c) express an imperative mood, while (8b) lacks it due to the absence of "tim1".

This analysis successfully captures the fact that in some cases, the post-verbal adverb, as the head, may carry more semantic-pragmatic meaning compared to the pre-verbal adverb. However, in this article, we take a different approach by examining the derivational relationship between the pre-verbal and post-verbal adverbs, particularly in the first type of circum-adverbial construction where they share the same phonetic form. Within the MP framework (see Chomsky 2000), we will explore the possibility of the pre-verbal adverb being derived through Internal Pair-Merge (IPM) of the post-verbal adverb. Building on Richards' (2009) argument that Pair-Merge should also have internal and external dimensions like Set-Merge, we can assume that the pre-verbal adverb in (1b) is derived from (1a) through IPM of the adverb "Kuɑ 33 'fast'". We will first present the derivation for the post-verbal case in (1a). "Kuɑ 33" is first merged inside VP. The complex verb "tɕʰy 2 -hɒ 33 'eat.PERF'" moves from V to v, as shown in (9a). The subject "ji13 'he/she'" merges at the Spec vP, and the object "ja21ve33 'dinner'" moves to the outer Spec vP, which serves as an intermediate landing site for A'-movement, as shown in (9b). Next, the subject "ji13" raises to Spec TP. The Sentence-final particle (SFP) "de," analyzed as a C head, merges with the TP, projecting a CP that expresses the speaker's affirmative mood. Both the subject "ji13" and the object "ja21ve33" are then topicalized, as in (9c). It's worth noting that "ji13" can also remain in Spec TP without being topicalized, as in (9d).
